text:
Applying the ideas concerning the process of wisdom formation as presented in Sir 6:18-22, the speaker of the following pericope emphasizes the necessity of submitting oneself completely to Wisdom and its effects (6:23-31). This segment is constructed in a chisastic format, consisting of three panels. The first and last panels, 6:23-25 and 6:29-31, describe slavery to Wisdom through images of bondage, while the second panel in the chiasmus, 6:26-28, uses the metaphor of hunting.

In the literary scheme of the section, the author is demonstrating a movement in the theology. The first section illustrates the painful aspects of the journey, focusing on the initial cost of surrender to freedom and self-determination, along with the heavy burden of obedience and the rigor of the discipline. When the author describes “the prison” of Wisdom in the final section, he surprisingly discovers the “chains” to be an “adorning” that testifies to the honor and glory that the patient student will enjoy in the future realm.

As far as Sirach is concerned, attaining Wisdom requires an amount of assiduity to which only a servant's obedience to his master could correspond, and which means that the ascent to Wisdom can in no way be a gradual process, nor be attempted in a non-exclusive fashion (as one also tends to try to do in the case of marriage, where one fears that exclusively devoted love will cause too many difficulties); it must be a continuous concern throughout one's entire life, since it is only the means of leading a life that is not only restful and cheerful but also famous and honourable with God. This is the reason why Sirach compares the search for Wisdom to the sovereign transition from servitude to kingly status.
